topic: Someone receives an invitation to their high school reunion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, cream-colored cardstock, bearing the embossed seal of the alumni association. Liam stared at it on his cluttered desk, the fluorescent light catching the sheen of the paper. A reunion. The date was six months away. He picked up a pen, not bothering to open it immediately. His gaze drifted toward the window, where the afternoon sun illuminated dust motes dancing in the stale air of his apartment. He considered the effort involved—the forced nostalgia, the awkward small talk. He finally tore it open, the sound surprisingly loud in the quiet room. Inside, the familiar names swam before his eyes. He skimmed over them, his thumb tracing the edges of a faded photograph of a group smiling too brightly. He folded the invitation neatly, placing it back in the stack, and turned his attention to a documentary on deep-sea vents.

[story 2]

"Another one," Sarah muttered, letting the invitation drop onto the coffee table with a soft thud. She didn't bother picking it up; she just watched it settle. The embossed lettering felt cold under her fingertips. She leaned back in her worn armchair, the springs groaning in protest. A flicker of something—a momentary recognition of the past—crossed her face, quickly smoothed over by a practiced neutrality. She picked up her phone, intending to text someone, but instead, she just scrolled through old photos on her gallery. The memory felt distant, like watching a movie someone else had made. She put the phone down, the screen going dark. The invitation remained untouched, a piece of paper signifying an obligation she had no immediate desire to fulfill.

[story 3]

Mark received the correspondence while waiting in line at the grocery store. He pulled out the glossy invitation, his expression utterly blank. He glanced at the embossed details, then looked up at the bustling crowd around him. People were laughing, hugging, clearly energized by the prospect of reconnecting. Mark felt a strange lack of pull toward that energy. He tucked the paper into his wallet, the weight of it negligible. He paid for his milk, his movements slow and deliberate. As he walked toward the exit, the invitation remained in his pocket, a small, irrelevant object in a sea of mundane errands. He didn't feel compelled to look at it again.
